question_text: The major scale follows which pattern of whole steps (W) and half steps (H)?
correct_answer: W-W-H-W-W-W-H
wrong_answer_1: W-H-W-W-H-W-W
wrong_answer_2: H-W-W-H-W-W-W
wrong_answer_3: W-W-W-H-W-W-H
explanation: The major scale formula is W-W-H-W-W-W-H. Two whole steps, a half step, then three whole steps and a final half step — creating the bright, familiar sound of the major scale.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.8; Kent Hewitt p.6

question_text: Which scale degree most clearly distinguishes a major scale from a minor scale?
correct_answer: The third degree (major 3rd vs minor 3rd)
wrong_answer_1: The fifth degree
wrong_answer_2: The seventh degree
wrong_answer_3: The second degree
explanation: The third degree is the primary distinguishing factor. A major 3rd (4 half steps) gives a bright quality; a minor 3rd (3 half steps) gives a darker quality.

question_text: Which mode is the first mode of the major scale — essentially the major scale itself?
correct_answer: Ionian
wrong_answer_1: Dorian
wrong_answer_2: Aeolian
wrong_answer_3: Lydian
explanation: Ionian is the 1st mode of the major scale. It starts on scale degree 1 and has the formula 1-2-3-4-5-6-7 — the standard major scale.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.16; Nahre Sol p.76

question_text: Playing all the white piano keys from D to D gives you which mode?
correct_answer: Dorian
wrong_answer_1: Phrygian
wrong_answer_2: Locrian
wrong_answer_3: Mixolydian
explanation: Dorian starts on D (the 2nd note of C major using only white keys). Formula: 1-2-b3-4-5-6-b7. It is a minor mode distinguished by its natural (raised) 6th.
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol p.76; Beato Book p.16

question_text: The Lydian mode is a major scale with which single alteration?
correct_answer: Raised 4th (#4)
wrong_answer_1: Lowered 7th (b7)
wrong_answer_2: Raised 5th (#5)
wrong_answer_3: Lowered 2nd (b2)
explanation: Lydian = major scale + #4 (raised 4th). Formula: 1-2-3-#4-5-6-7. The raised 4th gives it a bright, floating, dreamlike quality.
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol p.77; Beato Book p.16

question_text: The Mixolydian mode is the major scale with which single alteration?
correct_answer: Lowered 7th (b7)
wrong_answer_1: Raised 4th (#4)
wrong_answer_2: Lowered 6th (b6)
wrong_answer_3: Raised 2nd (#2)
explanation: Mixolydian = major scale + b7 (lowered 7th). Formula: 1-2-3-4-5-6-b7. This is the dominant mode — used on V7 chords in jazz.
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol p.77; Beato Book p.16

question_text: On which degree of the major scale does the Aeolian mode (natural minor) start?
correct_answer: 6th degree
wrong_answer_1: 2nd degree
wrong_answer_2: 3rd degree
wrong_answer_3: 7th degree
explanation: Aeolian (natural minor) starts on the 6th scale degree. For example, A Aeolian uses all the same notes as C major but starts and ends on A. Formula: 1-2-b3-4-5-b6-b7.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.16; Nahre Sol p.77

question_text: On which degree of the major scale does the Locrian mode start?
correct_answer: 7th degree
wrong_answer_1: 5th degree
wrong_answer_2: 3rd degree
wrong_answer_3: 6th degree
explanation: Locrian starts on the 7th scale degree. It is rarely used in practice because its tonic triad is diminished (b5), making it harmonically unstable. Formula: 1-b2-b3-4-b5-b6-b7.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.16; Nahre Sol p.76

question_text: A pentatonic scale has how many notes?
explanation: Penta' comes from the Greek word for five. The major pentatonic formula is 1-2-3-5-6. It removes the 4th and 7th from the major scale, eliminating the most tension-producing degrees.

question_text: The natural minor scale is identical to which mode?
correct_answer: Aeolian
wrong_answer_1: Dorian
wrong_answer_2: Phrygian
wrong_answer_3: Locrian
explanation: The Aeolian mode (6th mode of the major scale) is identical to the natural minor scale. A natural minor and C major share the same notes — they are relative keys.
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol p.75; Beato Book p.17

question_text: The harmonic minor scale differs from natural minor by raising which scale degree?
correct_answer: The 7th degree
wrong_answer_1: The 6th degree
wrong_answer_2: The 5th degree
wrong_answer_3: The 3rd degree
explanation: Harmonic minor raises the 7th degree by a half step, creating a leading tone (like in major). This also creates a distinctive augmented 2nd interval between scale degrees 6 and 7.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.19; Nahre Sol p.75

question_text: The ascending melodic minor scale raises which two degrees compared to natural minor?
correct_answer: The 6th and 7th degrees
wrong_answer_1: The 5th and 7th degrees
wrong_answer_2: The 3rd and 7th degrees
wrong_answer_3: The 6th and 5th degrees
explanation: Melodic minor (ascending) raises both the 6th and 7th: formula 1-2-b3-4-5-6-7. This avoids the awkward augmented 2nd of harmonic minor and creates a smoother melodic line upward.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.18; Nahre Sol p.75

question_text: Which two pairs of adjacent white piano keys have natural half steps (no black key between them)?
correct_answer: E-F and B-C
wrong_answer_1: A-B and C-D
wrong_answer_2: G-A and D-E
wrong_answer_3: C-D and F-G
explanation: E to F and B to C are the only natural half steps on the piano — there is no black key between them. All other adjacent white keys are a whole step apart.

question_text: A perfect fifth (P5) is described as having which characteristic quality?
correct_answer: Open Consonance
wrong_answer_1: Sharp Dissonance
wrong_answer_2: Soft Dissonance
wrong_answer_3: Neutral/Restless
explanation: The perfect fifth is one of the most stable intervals in music, producing an open consonance — stable and complete-sounding without needing to resolve.

question_text: The tritone (augmented 4th / diminished 5th) is described as having which quality?
correct_answer: Neutral/Restless
wrong_answer_1: Open Consonance
wrong_answer_2: Warm Consonance
wrong_answer_3: Soft Dissonance
explanation: The tritone divides the octave exactly in half. It is harmonically tense and strongly demands resolution — it sounds ambiguous and restless on its own.

question_text: In jazz, the 9th above the root is the same pitch class as which simpler interval?
correct_answer: Major 2nd
wrong_answer_1: Major 7th
wrong_answer_2: Perfect 5th
wrong_answer_3: Major 3rd
explanation: Upper extensions (9, 11, 13) are simply lower intervals displaced one octave higher. 9th = 2nd, 11th = 4th, 13th = 6th.

question_text: The 13th above the root is the same pitch class as which simpler interval?
correct_answer: Major 6th
wrong_answer_1: Minor 7th
wrong_answer_2: Perfect 5th
wrong_answer_3: Major 3rd
explanation: The 13th = 6th up an octave. In jazz chord symbols: 9th, 11th, and 13th are extensions that add color above the 7th of a chord.

question_text: How many half steps make up a perfect fifth?
explanation: A perfect fifth spans 7 half steps. For example: C-C#-D-D#-E-F-F#-G = 7 half steps from C to G.

question_text: A minor 2nd (one half step) is characterized as having which quality?
correct_answer: Sharp Dissonance
wrong_answer_1: Open Consonance
wrong_answer_2: Warm Consonance
wrong_answer_3: Soft Dissonance
explanation: The minor 2nd (1 half step) is the sharpest, most biting dissonance in Western harmony. It creates maximum tension and strongly demands resolution.

question_text: What does it mean for two notes to be 'enharmonic'?
correct_answer: They are the same pitch but spelled with different names (e.g. C# and Db)
wrong_answer_1: They are exactly one octave apart
wrong_answer_2: One note is altered by a half step from the other
wrong_answer_3: They form a dissonant interval
explanation: Enharmonic notes are played on the same piano key but written differently depending on the key signature context. C# and Db are the same pitch — enharmonic equivalents.

question_text: How many half steps make up a minor 7th interval?
explanation: A minor 7th spans 10 half steps — one half step less than a major 7th (11). Example: C to Bb = 10 half steps.

question_text: A major 3rd spans how many half steps?
explanation: A major 3rd = 4 half steps (e.g. C to E). A minor 3rd = 3 half steps (e.g. C to Eb). This one-half-step difference is what determines major vs minor chord quality.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.8; Kent Hewitt p.10

question_text: A minor 3rd spans how many half steps?
explanation: A minor 3rd = 3 half steps (e.g. C to Eb). This interval appears between the root and third of every minor and diminished chord.

question_text: How many half steps make up a major 7th?
explanation: A major 7th = 11 half steps — just one half step below the octave. Example: C to B = 11 half steps. This interval defines major 7th chords (Cmaj7).

question_text: What is the formula for a major triad?
explanation: A major triad: root + major 3rd (4 semitones) + perfect 5th. Formula: 1-3-5. Example: C major = C-E-G.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.14; Kent Hewitt p.18

question_text: What is the formula for a minor triad?
explanation: A minor triad: root + minor 3rd (3 semitones) + perfect 5th. Formula: 1-b3-5. Example: C minor = C-Eb-G.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.14; Kent Hewitt p.18

question_text: What is the formula for a diminished triad?
explanation: A diminished triad: root + minor 3rd + diminished 5th. Formula: 1-b3-b5. Built from two stacked minor 3rds (3+3 semitones), creating a tense, unstable sound.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.14; Kent Hewitt p.18

question_text: What is the formula for an augmented triad?
explanation: An augmented triad: root + major 3rd + augmented 5th. Formula: 1-3-#5. Built from two stacked major 3rds (4+4 semitones), creating a symmetrical, tense sound.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.14; Kent Hewitt p.18

question_text: A major triad consists of which two stacked interval types (from root upward)?
correct_answer: Major 3rd then minor 3rd (M3 + m3)
wrong_answer_1: Two major 3rds (M3 + M3)
wrong_answer_2: Two minor 3rds (m3 + m3)
wrong_answer_3: Minor 3rd then major 3rd (m3 + M3)
explanation: All triads are stacked thirds. Major = M3 (4 st) + m3 (3 st). Minor = m3 + M3. Diminished = m3 + m3. Augmented = M3 + M3.
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt p.18; Beato Book p.27

question_text: When a triad is in first inversion, which note is in the bass?
correct_answer: The third
wrong_answer_1: The root
wrong_answer_2: The fifth
wrong_answer_3: The seventh
explanation: First inversion: the third is in the bass. For C major (C-E-G): root position = C bass; 1st inversion = E in bass; 2nd inversion = G in bass.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.27; Kent Hewitt p.30

question_text: When a triad is in second inversion, which note is in the bass?
correct_answer: The fifth
wrong_answer_1: The third
wrong_answer_2: The root
wrong_answer_3: The seventh
explanation: Second inversion places the fifth in the bass. C major 2nd inversion = G-C-E (the fifth G is lowest). Written in slash notation as C/G.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.27; Kent Hewitt p.30

question_text: In slash chord notation, a first inversion C major chord is written as:
correct_answer: C/E
wrong_answer_1: C/G
wrong_answer_2: E/C
wrong_answer_3: G/C
explanation: Slash chords show the chord quality left of the slash and the bass note right of the slash. C/E = C major with E in the bass (1st inversion). C/G = 2nd inversion.

question_text: A suspended chord (sus) replaces which note from a basic triad?
correct_answer: The third
wrong_answer_1: The fifth
wrong_answer_2: The root
wrong_answer_3: The seventh
explanation: Sus chords replace the third with the 4th (sus4) or 2nd (sus2). The absence of the third creates an unresolved, open quality — the suspended note wants to resolve to the 3rd.

question_text: A sus4 chord replaces the third with which interval above the root?
correct_answer: Perfect 4th
wrong_answer_1: Augmented 4th
wrong_answer_2: Major 2nd
wrong_answer_3: Minor 2nd
explanation: Csus4 = C-F-G (the E is raised to F, a perfect 4th above root). The 4th naturally wants to resolve down a half step to the 3rd — hence 'suspended.'
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.29; Kent Hewitt p.19

question_text: A sus4 chord contains which two intervals above the root?
correct_answer: Perfect 4th and perfect 5th
wrong_answer_1: Major 3rd and perfect 5th
wrong_answer_2: Perfect 4th and major 6th
wrong_answer_3: Minor 3rd and perfect 4th
explanation: A sus4 chord (e.g. Csus4 = C-F-G) contains a P4 and P5 above the root. The 3rd is completely omitted, giving the chord a harmonically open, unresolved sound.

question_text: In C major, the chord built on the 4th scale degree (F) is what quality?
correct_answer: Major
wrong_answer_1: Minor
wrong_answer_2: Diminished
wrong_answer_3: Augmented
explanation: Diatonic triads in C major: I=C(M), ii=Dm, iii=Em, IV=F(M), V=G(M), vi=Am, vii°=Bdim. The IV chord is always major in any major key.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.13; Kent Hewitt p.23

question_text: In C major, the chord built on the 2nd scale degree (D) is what quality?
correct_answer: Minor
wrong_answer_1: Major
wrong_answer_2: Diminished
wrong_answer_3: Augmented
explanation: The ii chord is always minor in a major key. In C major: Dm (D-F-A). The supertonic (ii) commonly functions as a Pre-Dominant chord, leading to V.
sources_and_notes: Beato Book p.13; Kent Hewitt p.23

question_text: The V chord naturally resolves to which chord?
correct_answer: The I chord (Tonic)
wrong_answer_1: The IV chord (Subdominant)
wrong_answer_2: The ii chord (Supertonic)
wrong_answer_3: The vi chord (Submediant)
explanation: The V-I (Dominant to Tonic) resolution is the strongest harmonic motion in Western music. The dominant chord creates tension that naturally resolves to the tonic.
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt p.26; Beato Book p.37

question_text: What are the three functional chord categories in tonal harmony?
correct_answer: Tonic, Pre-Dominant, and Dominant
wrong_answer_1: Major, Minor, and Diminished
wrong_answer_2: Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary
wrong_answer_3: Stable, Unstable, and Transitional
explanation: Functional harmony organizes chords by role: Tonic (I, iii, vi) = at rest; Pre-Dominant (IV, ii) = preparing movement; Dominant (V, vii°) = creating tension that resolves to Tonic.

question_text: Tonic chords are defined as chords that do NOT contain which scale degree?
correct_answer: The 4th degree
wrong_answer_1: The 7th degree
wrong_answer_2: The 5th degree
wrong_answer_3: The 1st degree
explanation: Tonic chords (I, iii, vi) all lack the 4th scale degree. The 4th degree is the defining element of Pre-Dominant chords — its absence gives Tonic chords their stability.

question_text: Which chord is a Dominant function chord because it contains both the 5th and 7th scale degrees?
correct_answer: V7 (e.g. G7 in C major)
wrong_answer_1: IVmaj7 (e.g. Fmaj7 in C major)
wrong_answer_2: Imaj7 (e.g. Cmaj7 in C major)
wrong_answer_3: ii7 (e.g. Dm7 in C major)
explanation: Dominant chords (V7 and vii°7b5) contain both the 5th AND 7th degrees of the scale — the two least stable tones. The 4th degree resolves down and the 7th resolves up to the tonic triad.

question_text: The 1st scale degree is called which name?
correct_answer: Tonic
wrong_answer_1: Dominant
wrong_answer_2: Subdominant
wrong_answer_3: Mediant
explanation: Scale degree names: 1=Tonic, 2=Supertonic, 3=Mediant, 4=Subdominant, 5=Dominant, 6=Submediant, 7=Leading Tone (major) or Subtonic (natural minor).

question_text: The 4th scale degree is called which name?
correct_answer: Subdominant
wrong_answer_1: Supertonic
wrong_answer_2: Submediant
wrong_answer_3: Mediant
explanation: The subdominant (4th degree) is literally 'below the dominant.' It forms the basis of the IV chord and is the defining tone of Pre-Dominant function in tonal harmony.

question_text: In a major scale, the 7th degree (leading tone) has a strong tendency to resolve up by a half step to which note?
correct_answer: The 1st degree (Tonic)
wrong_answer_1: The 5th degree (Dominant)
wrong_answer_2: The 4th degree (Subdominant)
wrong_answer_3: The 3rd degree (Mediant)
explanation: The leading tone is just a half step below the tonic, creating strong upward pull. In C major, B resolves to C. This resolution is fundamental to all authentic cadences.

question_text: On the Cycle of 5ths, in which direction does dominant-to-tonic resolution move?
correct_answer: Counter-clockwise
wrong_answer_1: Clockwise
wrong_answer_2: Inward toward the center
wrong_answer_3: Outward from the center
explanation: Moving counter-clockwise on the Cycle of 5ths represents resolution — dominant to tonic. G is one step clockwise from C, so G7→C moves counter-clockwise.
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt p.24; Beato Book p.12

question_text: The II-V-I progression in C major uses which three chords?
correct_answer: Dm7 - G7 - Cmaj7
wrong_answer_1: Fm - C - G
wrong_answer_2: Am - D - G
wrong_answer_3: Em - A - D
explanation: The II-V-I is the cornerstone of jazz harmony. In C: Dm7 (ii7) → G7 (V7) → Cmaj7 (I). The ii pushes to V which resolves to I — three steps of the functional chain.
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt p.29; Beato Book p.39

question_text: Moving smoothly between chords with minimal voice movement — using inversions to avoid large leaps — is called what?
correct_answer: Voice Leading (or Economy of Motion)
wrong_answer_1: Contrary Motion
wrong_answer_2: Parallel Motion
wrong_answer_3: Voice Crossing
explanation: Voice leading and economy of motion describe moving each voice the shortest distance possible between chords. Inversions are the primary tool for achieving this smooth movement.

question_text: Which chords belong to the Pre-Dominant function?
correct_answer: IV and ii
wrong_answer_1: I, iii, and vi
wrong_answer_2: V and vii°
wrong_answer_3: IV and V
explanation: Pre-Dominant chords (IV and ii) contain the 4th scale degree. They prepare the move to Dominant. Progressions typically flow: Tonic → Pre-Dominant → Dominant → Tonic.

question_text: In a major key, the Tonic function group contains which chords?
correct_answer: I, iii, and vi
wrong_answer_1: IV and ii
wrong_answer_2: V and vii°
wrong_answer_3: I and IV
explanation: Tonic chords (I, iii, vi) share a sense of rest and stability. They all lack the 4th scale degree. Movement within the Tonic group is harmonically stable before pushing forward.

question_text: The 6th scale degree is called which name?
correct_answer: Submediant
wrong_answer_1: Mediant
wrong_answer_2: Supertonic
wrong_answer_3: Subdominant
explanation: Scale degree 6 = Submediant (below the mediant). In minor keys, a major chord on the submediant (bVI) is commonly borrowed for color and movement.

question_text: What does a time signature tell you?
correct_answer: How many beats are in each measure and what note value gets one beat
wrong_answer_1: The tempo of a piece
wrong_answer_2: The key of a piece
wrong_answer_3: The dynamic level
explanation: The top number indicates beats per measure; the bottom number indicates which note value equals one beat.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: In 4/4 time, how many beats are in each measure?
explanation: The top number '4' in 4/4 time means there are 4 beats per measure.

question_text: How many beats does a whole note receive in 4/4 time?
correct_answer: 4 beats
wrong_answer_1: 1 beat
wrong_answer_2: 2 beats
wrong_answer_3: 3 beats
explanation: A whole note lasts the full measure in 4/4 time, receiving 4 beats.

question_text: How many eighth notes are equal to one quarter note?
explanation: One quarter note divides into exactly 2 eighth notes.

question_text: How many beats is a dotted quarter note worth in 4/4 time?
correct_answer: 1.5 beats
wrong_answer_1: 2 beats
wrong_answer_2: 1 beat
wrong_answer_3: 3 beats
explanation: A dot adds half the note's value. A quarter note = 1 beat, so dotted quarter = 1.5 beats.

question_text: What is the musical term for a period of silence?
correct_answer: Rest
wrong_answer_1: Pause
wrong_answer_2: Fermata
wrong_answer_3: Hold
explanation: A rest indicates a period of silence. Each note value has a corresponding rest symbol.

question_text: In 3/4 time, how many quarter-note beats are in each measure?
explanation: The top number '3' in 3/4 means 3 beats per measure; the bottom '4' means the quarter note gets one beat.

question_text: What is the 'downbeat' in a measure?
correct_answer: The first beat of a measure
wrong_answer_1: The last beat of a measure
wrong_answer_2: A weak beat
wrong_answer_3: A beat played softly
explanation: The downbeat is beat 1 of a measure, typically the strongest and most emphasized beat.

question_text: How many sixteenth notes equal one quarter note?
explanation: One quarter note subdivides into 4 sixteenth notes.

question_text: What does a fermata symbol tell the performer?
correct_answer: Hold the note longer than its written value
wrong_answer_1: Play the note shorter
wrong_answer_2: Repeat the note
wrong_answer_3: Play the note louder
explanation: A fermata (bird's eye symbol) tells the performer to hold the note for an extended duration at the performer's or conductor's discretion.

question_text: What type of meter has beats that naturally divide into groups of three?
correct_answer: Compound meter
wrong_answer_1: Simple meter
wrong_answer_2: Duple meter
wrong_answer_3: Triple meter
explanation: In compound meter (e.g. 6/8), the beat divides into three equal parts. In simple meter, the beat divides into two.

question_text: What is syncopation in music?
correct_answer: Accenting a normally weak beat or placing emphasis on the off-beat
wrong_answer_1: Playing two notes at the same time
wrong_answer_2: Playing a note louder than the others
wrong_answer_3: Gradually speeding up the tempo
explanation: Syncopation creates rhythmic interest and energy by placing emphasis on beats that are normally unaccented.
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol Elements of Music; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What does musical 'form' describe?
correct_answer: The overall structure or organization of a piece of music
wrong_answer_1: The speed of a piece
wrong_answer_2: The key of a piece
wrong_answer_3: The instrumentation of a piece
explanation: Musical form describes how sections of a piece are arranged, such as verse, chorus, and bridge in a pop song.

question_text: What letter is traditionally used to label the first distinct section in formal analysis?
correct_answer: A
wrong_answer_1: B
wrong_answer_2: C
wrong_answer_3: I
explanation: In formal analysis, the first distinct section is labeled 'A.' Contrasting new sections receive new letters (B, C, etc.).

question_text: What is binary form?
correct_answer: A musical form with two main contrasting sections (AB)
wrong_answer_1: A form with three sections
wrong_answer_2: A form that repeats exactly throughout
wrong_answer_3: A form with an introduction and a coda only
explanation: Binary form (AB) consists of two distinct sections, often both repeated.

question_text: What is ternary form?
correct_answer: A three-part structure where the opening section returns at the end (ABA)
wrong_answer_1: A form with four sections
wrong_answer_2: A form with only one section
wrong_answer_3: A form where no section ever repeats
explanation: Ternary form (ABA) presents a statement, a contrasting section, and then a return to the opening material.

question_text: In a pop song, what does the 'verse' typically do?
correct_answer: Tells the story of the song, usually with different lyrics each time
wrong_answer_1: Contains the catchiest repeated hook
wrong_answer_2: Provides an instrumental break
wrong_answer_3: Serves as the final closing section
explanation: Verses advance the narrative with changing lyrics each time while sharing the same melody.

question_text: In a pop song, what is the 'chorus'?
correct_answer: The repeated section with the same lyrics and melody, usually the main hook
wrong_answer_1: The opening instrumental section
wrong_answer_2: A section reserved for improvisation
wrong_answer_3: The contrasting bridge section
explanation: The chorus is the most recognizable and repeated section, typically containing the song's title or main emotional message.

question_text: What is the purpose of a 'bridge' in a song?
correct_answer: To provide contrast and variety before returning to familiar material
wrong_answer_1: To open the song
wrong_answer_2: To serve as the final verse
wrong_answer_3: To repeat the main hook
explanation: The bridge offers a fresh perspective or harmonic contrast, breaking up the verse-chorus pattern.

question_text: What is a musical 'phrase'?
correct_answer: A musical sentence that expresses a complete musical idea
wrong_answer_1: A single note value
wrong_answer_2: A chord progression
wrong_answer_3: An entire composition
explanation: A phrase is a musical unit analogous to a sentence in language — it has a beginning, middle, and a sense of conclusion.

question_text: What is an 'intro' in a song?
correct_answer: The opening section before the main content begins
wrong_answer_1: The repeated final section
wrong_answer_2: The improvisation section
wrong_answer_3: A middle contrasting section
explanation: An intro sets up the mood and key of a song before the verse or chorus begins.

question_text: What is a 'coda' in music?
correct_answer: A concluding section added to the end of a piece to create finality
wrong_answer_1: The opening theme of a piece
wrong_answer_2: A repeated middle section
wrong_answer_3: A sudden key change
explanation: A coda (Italian for 'tail') provides a sense of closure and finality at the end of a composition.

question_text: What is a 'theme' in the context of classical music?
correct_answer: A recognizable melodic idea that forms the basis of a composition
wrong_answer_1: A tempo marking
wrong_answer_2: A key signature
wrong_answer_3: A time signature choice
explanation: A theme is a central musical idea that is developed, varied, and contrasted throughout a composition.

question_text: What does 'D.C. al Fine' instruct the performer to do?
correct_answer: Return to the beginning and play until the marking 'Fine'
wrong_answer_1: Skip to the end of the piece
wrong_answer_2: Repeat only from the sign
wrong_answer_3: Gradually slow down to a stop
explanation: Da Capo al Fine' means go back to the very beginning ('Da Capo') and stop at the word 'Fine.'

question_text: How many measures does the standard 12-bar blues form last?
explanation: The 12-bar blues is one of the most common forms in jazz and blues, consisting of a 12-measure harmonic pattern often built on I, IV, and V chords.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What is a dominant seventh chord?
correct_answer: A major triad with a minor seventh added
wrong_answer_1: A minor triad with a major seventh
wrong_answer_2: A major triad with a major seventh
wrong_answer_3: A diminished triad with a seventh
explanation: The dominant 7th chord (e.g. G7) is built with a major triad plus a minor (flatted) seventh and is one of the most important chords in jazz.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What is the 'II-V-I' chord progression in jazz?
correct_answer: A progression moving from the 2nd to 5th to 1st scale degree chords
wrong_answer_1: A scale with two flats
wrong_answer_2: A specific rhythmic pattern used in jazz
wrong_answer_3: A type of jazz improvisation technique
explanation: The II-V-I (e.g. Dm7-G7-Cmaj7 in C major) is the most fundamental harmonic motion in jazz.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What defines a 'swing feel' in jazz?
correct_answer: Pairs of eighth notes are played unevenly — the first note is stretched and the second is shortened
wrong_answer_1: All notes are played with perfectly equal duration
wrong_answer_2: The music is played at a very slow tempo
wrong_answer_3: The music has a strong backbeat on beats 2 and 4
explanation: In swing, eighth note pairs have a long-short relationship, creating a lilting, bouncing feel central to jazz style.
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol Elements of Music; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is the 'root' of a chord?
correct_answer: The note on which the chord is built and from which it takes its name
wrong_answer_1: The highest note of the chord
wrong_answer_2: The middle note of the chord
wrong_answer_3: An optional decorative note in the chord
explanation: The root is the foundational note. In a C major chord, C is the root.

question_text: Which scale is most naturally associated with dominant 7th chords?
correct_answer: Mixolydian
wrong_answer_1: Dorian
wrong_answer_2: Lydian
wrong_answer_3: Phrygian
explanation: The Mixolydian mode (a major scale with a flatted 7th) fits naturally over dominant 7th chords.

question_text: What is a 'jazz standard'?
correct_answer: A well-known composition widely performed and recognized in the jazz repertoire
wrong_answer_1: A strict set of rules for jazz improvisation
wrong_answer_2: A specific type of jazz rhythm
wrong_answer_3: A particular chord progression used in all jazz
explanation: Jazz standards are songs from the Great American Songbook and jazz tradition that musicians learn as a shared repertoire.

question_text: What does 'voicing' refer to in jazz harmony?
correct_answer: The specific arrangement and distribution of notes within a chord
wrong_answer_1: The style of the jazz vocalist
wrong_answer_2: The overall loudness of a chord
wrong_answer_3: The speed at which chords change
explanation: Voicing describes which notes are in a chord, what order they appear in, and how they are spaced across registers.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What interval is formed between the 3rd and 7th of a dominant seventh chord?
correct_answer: A tritone (augmented 4th / diminished 5th)
wrong_answer_1: A perfect fifth
wrong_answer_2: A major third
wrong_answer_3: A minor second
explanation: The tritone between the 3rd and 7th of a dominant chord creates strong tension that wants to resolve to the tonic chord.

question_text: What is a major 7th chord (maj7)?
correct_answer: A major triad with a major seventh added
wrong_answer_1: A major triad with a minor seventh
wrong_answer_2: A minor triad with a major seventh
wrong_answer_3: A triad with an added 9th only
explanation: A major 7th chord (e.g. Cmaj7) consists of a major triad (C-E-G) plus a major seventh interval (B).
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is a minor 7th chord (min7)?
correct_answer: A minor triad with a minor seventh added
wrong_answer_1: A minor triad with a major seventh
wrong_answer_2: A major triad with a minor seventh
wrong_answer_3: A diminished triad with a seventh
explanation: A minor 7th chord (e.g. Dm7) consists of a minor triad (D-F-A) plus a minor seventh (C).
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: In jazz, what does 'comping' mean?
correct_answer: Accompanying a soloist with chords and rhythmic patterns
wrong_answer_1: Improvising a melodic solo
wrong_answer_2: Playing the bass line
wrong_answer_3: Counting the tempo for the band
explanation: Comping' (short for accompanying) is the practice of playing supportive, rhythmically varied chords behind a soloist.

question_text: What is a 'turnaround' in jazz?
correct_answer: A chord progression at the end of a section that leads back to the beginning
wrong_answer_1: A modulation to a new key
wrong_answer_2: A sudden change in tempo
wrong_answer_3: A rhythmic drum fill
explanation: A turnaround (e.g. I-VI-II-V) appears at the end of a section to create harmonic momentum back to the top.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: How many sharps are in the key of G major?
explanation: G major has one sharp: F#.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; General music theory

question_text: What is the one sharp found in the key of G major?
correct_answer: F#
wrong_answer_1: C#
wrong_answer_2: G#
wrong_answer_3: B#
explanation: G major's key signature contains only F#, which raises the 7th scale degree.

question_text: How many flats are in the key of F major?
explanation: F major has one flat: Bb.

question_text: What is the one flat found in the key of F major?
correct_answer: Bb
wrong_answer_1: Eb
wrong_answer_2: Ab
wrong_answer_3: Db
explanation: F major's key signature contains only Bb, which lowers the 4th scale degree.

question_text: How many sharps or flats does C major have?
correct_answer: None — no sharps or flats
wrong_answer_1: 1 sharp
wrong_answer_2: 1 flat
wrong_answer_3: 2 sharps
explanation: C major is the only major key with no sharps or flats in its key signature.

question_text: What is the relative minor of C major?
correct_answer: A minor
wrong_answer_1: E minor
wrong_answer_2: D minor
wrong_answer_3: G minor
explanation: The relative minor starts on the 6th scale degree of the major scale. The 6th degree of C major is A, so A minor is relative to C major.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; General music theory

question_text: How many sharps are in the key of D major?
explanation: D major has two sharps: F# and C#.

question_text: Which two sharps appear in the key of D major?
correct_answer: F# and C#
wrong_answer_1: F# and G#
wrong_answer_2: C# and G#
wrong_answer_3: F# and B#
explanation: D major raises the 3rd scale degree (F#) and the 7th scale degree (C#).

question_text: How many flats are in the key of Bb major?
explanation: Bb major has two flats: Bb and Eb.

question_text: What is the Circle of Fifths (also called the Cycle of Fifths)?
correct_answer: A diagram showing all 12 keys arranged by intervals of a fifth
wrong_answer_1: A method of counting subdivisions
wrong_answer_2: A type of repeating chord progression
wrong_answer_3: A pentatonic scale pattern
explanation: The Circle/Cycle of Fifths organizes all 12 major and minor keys so adjacent keys are a perfect fifth apart, making it easy to see key relationships and accidentals.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: Moving clockwise on the Circle of Fifths, what happens to the key signature?
correct_answer: Each new key gains one more sharp (or loses one flat)
wrong_answer_1: Each new key gains one more flat
wrong_answer_2: Keys alternate between sharps and flats with each step
wrong_answer_3: The key signature stays the same
explanation: Moving clockwise (up a perfect fifth) adds one sharp. Moving counterclockwise (up a perfect fourth) adds one flat.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What is a 'relative minor' key?
correct_answer: A minor key that shares the same key signature as a major key but has a different tonic
wrong_answer_1: A minor key one half step below a major key
wrong_answer_2: A minor key with no sharps or flats
wrong_answer_3: A minor key that starts on the same root note as a major key
explanation: Relative keys share the same key signature but different tonic notes (e.g. G major and E minor both have one sharp: F#).

question_text: What is a 'parallel minor' key?
correct_answer: A minor key that shares the same root note as a major key but has a different key signature
wrong_answer_1: A minor key with the same key signature as its major counterpart
wrong_answer_2: A minor key a perfect fifth away from a major key
wrong_answer_3: A minor key with no accidentals
explanation: Parallel keys share the same starting note but have different key signatures (e.g. C major and C minor are parallel keys).

question_text: What is the Dorian mode?
correct_answer: A natural minor scale with a raised 6th
wrong_answer_1: A major scale with a lowered 7th
wrong_answer_2: A major scale with a raised 4th
wrong_answer_3: A natural minor scale with a lowered 5th
explanation: Dorian (2nd mode of the major scale) has the formula W-H-W-W-W-H-W, identical to natural minor but with a raised (major) 6th.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: What is the Mixolydian mode?
correct_answer: A major scale with a lowered 7th
wrong_answer_1: A minor scale with a raised 6th
wrong_answer_2: A major scale with a raised 4th
wrong_answer_3: A major scale with a lowered 3rd
explanation: Mixolydian (5th mode of the major scale) is identical to a major scale except its 7th degree is lowered by a half step.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: What is the Lydian mode?
correct_answer: A major scale with a raised 4th
wrong_answer_1: A major scale with a lowered 7th
wrong_answer_2: A minor scale with a raised 6th
wrong_answer_3: A major scale with a lowered 3rd
explanation: Lydian (4th mode of the major scale) is like a major scale with its 4th degree raised by a half step, creating an augmented 4th from the tonic.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: What is the Phrygian mode?
correct_answer: A natural minor scale with a lowered 2nd
wrong_answer_1: A minor scale with a raised 6th
wrong_answer_2: A major scale with a raised 4th
wrong_answer_3: A minor scale with a raised 7th
explanation: Phrygian (3rd mode of the major scale) resembles natural minor but has a characteristic flatted 2nd scale degree, giving it a dark, exotic sound.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: What is the Locrian mode?
correct_answer: A natural minor scale with both a lowered 2nd and a lowered 5th
wrong_answer_1: A minor scale with a raised 6th
wrong_answer_2: A major scale with a lowered 7th
wrong_answer_3: A minor scale with only a raised 7th
explanation: Locrian (7th mode of the major scale) has a b2 and b5, producing a diminished quality. It is the only diatonic mode with a diminished 5th above the root.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: D Dorian uses only natural notes (no sharps or flats). Which major key does D Dorian come from?
correct_answer: C major
wrong_answer_1: G major
wrong_answer_2: F major
wrong_answer_3: D major
explanation: D Dorian is the 2nd mode of C major — it uses exactly the same notes as C major but treats D as the tonal center.

question_text: What is the melodic minor scale?
correct_answer: A natural minor scale with both a raised 6th and raised 7th when ascending
wrong_answer_1: A natural minor scale with only a raised 7th
wrong_answer_2: An unchanged natural minor scale
wrong_answer_3: A major scale with a lowered 3rd
explanation: The ascending melodic minor raises the 6th and 7th to smooth out the awkward augmented 2nd found in harmonic minor.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: What is the harmonic minor scale?
correct_answer: A natural minor scale with a raised 7th
wrong_answer_1: A minor scale with both a raised 6th and 7th
wrong_answer_2: A minor scale with a lowered 2nd
wrong_answer_3: An unchanged natural minor scale
explanation: The harmonic minor raises only the 7th scale degree, creating a leading tone and a characteristic augmented 2nd between the 6th and 7th degrees.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: Which chord type does the Lydian mode naturally fit over?
correct_answer: Major 7th with a sharp 11 (maj7#11)
wrong_answer_1: Dominant 7th
wrong_answer_2: Minor 7th
wrong_answer_3: Half-diminished
explanation: Lydian's raised 4th creates a #11 above the root, making it the ideal scale for major chords with a sharp 11 extension.

question_text: Playing only the black keys on a piano produces which type of scale?
correct_answer: A pentatonic scale (Gb major / Eb minor pentatonic)
wrong_answer_1: The chromatic scale
wrong_answer_2: The whole tone scale
wrong_answer_3: The blues scale
explanation: The five black keys of a piano form a pentatonic scale — specifically Gb major pentatonic or its relative, Eb minor pentatonic.

question_text: How is the major pentatonic scale derived from the major scale?
correct_answer: By removing the 4th and 7th scale degrees
wrong_answer_1: By removing the 3rd and 6th
wrong_answer_2: By adding a flatted 3rd to the major scale
wrong_answer_3: By raising the 4th scale degree
explanation: The major pentatonic uses scale degrees 1, 2, 3, 5, and 6 of the major scale, omitting the 4th and 7th (the two 'avoid notes').
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol Elements of Music; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is a 'tetrachord'?
correct_answer: A group of four consecutive notes spanning a perfect fourth
wrong_answer_1: A four-note chord
wrong_answer_2: A scale made up of only four notes
wrong_answer_3: A group of four measures
explanation: A tetrachord is a four-note stepwise sequence spanning a perfect fourth. Two tetrachords joined together form a major scale.
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol Elements of Music; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: The Lydian Dominant scale is the 4th mode of which scale?
correct_answer: The melodic minor scale
wrong_answer_1: The harmonic minor scale
wrong_answer_2: The major scale
wrong_answer_3: The natural minor scale
explanation: Lydian Dominant (mode 4 of melodic minor) has a raised 4th and flatted 7th, making it ideal for dominant 7th chords with a #11.

question_text: What is a compound interval?
correct_answer: An interval larger than an octave
wrong_answer_1: An interval of exactly one octave
wrong_answer_2: Two notes played at the same time
wrong_answer_3: An interval smaller than a half step
explanation: Compound intervals exceed an octave. For example, a 9th is a compound interval — it is an octave plus a 2nd.

question_text: What is the inversion of a major 3rd?
correct_answer: A minor 6th
wrong_answer_1: A major 6th
wrong_answer_2: A minor 3rd
wrong_answer_3: A perfect 4th
explanation: When an interval is inverted, major becomes minor (and vice versa), and the two interval numbers add up to 9. A major 3rd (3) inverts to a minor 6th (6).

question_text: What is the inversion of a perfect 5th?
correct_answer: A perfect 4th
wrong_answer_1: A perfect 5th
wrong_answer_2: A major 4th
wrong_answer_3: A minor 6th
explanation: Perfect intervals invert to perfect intervals. A perfect 5th (5) + perfect 4th (4) = 9, so a P5 inverts to a P4.

question_text: What are 'enharmonic intervals'?
correct_answer: Two intervals that sound the same but are written with different note names
wrong_answer_1: Two identical intervals in different octaves
wrong_answer_2: An interval that spans exactly one octave
wrong_answer_3: An interval that contains no half steps
explanation: An augmented 4th (C to F#) and a diminished 5th (C to Gb) are enharmonically equivalent — they sound identical but are spelled differently.

question_text: What is the interval from C up to D#?
correct_answer: Augmented 2nd
wrong_answer_1: Minor 3rd
wrong_answer_2: Major 2nd
wrong_answer_3: Diminished 3rd
explanation: C to D is a major 2nd. Raising D to D# augments the interval — it spans 3 half steps like a minor 3rd but is written as an augmented 2nd.

question_text: What is a 'chromatic half step'?
correct_answer: A half step between two notes with the same letter name (e.g. C to C#)
wrong_answer_1: A half step between two notes with different letter names
wrong_answer_2: The smallest possible interval in any tuning system
wrong_answer_3: Two notes with the exact same pitch
explanation: A chromatic half step uses the same letter name with an altered accidental (e.g. E to E#). A diatonic half step uses consecutive letter names (e.g. E to F).

question_text: How many half steps make up a perfect 4th?
explanation: A perfect 4th spans 5 half steps (e.g. C up to F).

question_text: What interval exists between the 1st and 7th scale degrees of a major scale?
correct_answer: Major 7th
wrong_answer_1: Minor 7th
wrong_answer_2: Perfect 7th
wrong_answer_3: Augmented 6th
explanation: The 7th scale degree of a major scale is a major 7th (11 half steps) above the tonic.

question_text: What interval exists between the 1st and 7th scale degrees of a natural minor scale?
correct_answer: Minor 7th
wrong_answer_1: Major 7th
wrong_answer_2: Diminished 7th
wrong_answer_3: Perfect 7th
explanation: In natural minor, the 7th scale degree is a minor 7th (10 half steps) above the root.

question_text: How many half steps make up an augmented 4th (tritone)?
explanation: The tritone spans exactly 6 half steps, dividing the octave precisely in half. It can be written as an augmented 4th or diminished 5th.

question_text: What is the interval from G up to E?
correct_answer: Major 6th
wrong_answer_1: Minor 6th
wrong_answer_2: Perfect 6th
wrong_answer_3: Minor 7th
explanation: G to E spans 6 letter names (G-A-B-C-D-E) and 9 half steps, making it a major 6th.

question_text: How are intervals classified as consonant or dissonant?
correct_answer: Consonant intervals sound stable and at rest; dissonant intervals sound tense and unstable
wrong_answer_1: Consonant intervals are louder; dissonant intervals are softer
wrong_answer_2: Consonant intervals are larger; dissonant intervals are smaller
wrong_answer_3: Consonant intervals are fast; dissonant intervals are held longer
explanation: Perfect 5ths and major 3rds are consonant (stable), while tritones and major 7ths are dissonant, seeking resolution.

question_text: What interval is formed between the 3rd and 7th of a dominant seventh chord?
correct_answer: Diminished 5th (tritone)
wrong_answer_1: Perfect 5th
wrong_answer_2: Major 3rd
wrong_answer_3: Minor 6th
explanation: In a G7 chord (G-B-D-F), the 3rd (B) to the 7th (F) is a diminished 5th — a tritone that creates powerful tension demanding resolution.

question_text: What is a seventh chord?
correct_answer: A triad with a seventh interval added on top
wrong_answer_1: A chord with only two notes
wrong_answer_2: A chord built entirely in fourths
wrong_answer_3: A triad played in inversion
explanation: Seventh chords are four-note chords: root, 3rd, 5th, and 7th. They are fundamental in jazz and classical harmony.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What are the four basic types of seventh chords?
correct_answer: Major 7th, minor 7th, dominant 7th, and minor 7b5 (half-diminished)
wrong_answer_1: Major 7th, minor 7th, augmented 7th, and suspended 7th
wrong_answer_2: Major 7th, minor 7th, diminished 7th, and Lydian 7th
wrong_answer_3: Dominant 7th, augmented 7th, suspended 7th, and quartal 7th
explanation: The four standard seventh chords are maj7, min7, dominant 7 (maj-min 7), and half-diminished (min7b5).
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What is a sus4 chord?
correct_answer: A chord where the 3rd is replaced by the perfect 4th above the root
wrong_answer_1: A chord with an added 4th alongside the 3rd
wrong_answer_2: A chord with a flatted 4th
wrong_answer_3: A chord where the 5th is replaced by the 4th
explanation: In a sus4 chord, the 3rd is suspended (removed) and replaced by the 4th scale degree, creating harmonic tension that typically resolves back to the 3rd.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What is a sus2 chord?
correct_answer: A chord where the 3rd is replaced by the major 2nd above the root
wrong_answer_1: A chord with an added 2nd alongside the 3rd
wrong_answer_2: A chord with a flatted 2nd
wrong_answer_3: A chord where the 5th is replaced by the 2nd
explanation: In a sus2 chord, the 3rd is replaced by the major 2nd, producing an open, ambiguous sound without a clear major or minor quality.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What does a slash chord notation like 'C/E' indicate?
correct_answer: A C major chord played with E in the bass
wrong_answer_1: A chord played very quickly
wrong_answer_2: A chord with a cut-time rhythmic feel
wrong_answer_3: A chord played with the right hand only
explanation: In slash chord notation, the letter before the slash is the chord and the letter after is the bass note. C/E is a C major chord with E as the lowest note.

question_text: What is a 'first inversion' triad?
correct_answer: A triad with its 3rd as the lowest (bass) note
wrong_answer_1: A triad with its root as the lowest note
wrong_answer_2: A triad with its 5th as the lowest note
wrong_answer_3: A triad with its 7th as the lowest note
explanation: In first inversion, the 3rd of the chord is in the bass. For C major (C-E-G), first inversion is E-G-C.
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is a 'second inversion' triad?
correct_answer: A triad with its 5th as the lowest (bass) note
wrong_answer_1: A triad with its root as the lowest note
wrong_answer_2: A triad with its 3rd as the lowest note
wrong_answer_3: A triad with its 7th as the lowest note
explanation: In second inversion, the 5th of the chord is in the bass. For C major (C-E-G), second inversion is G-C-E.
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is an augmented triad?
correct_answer: A major triad with the 5th raised by a half step
wrong_answer_1: A minor triad with a perfect 5th
wrong_answer_2: A major triad with a perfect 5th
wrong_answer_3: A chord built from two minor 3rds
explanation: An augmented triad (e.g. C-E-G#) is built with a major 3rd + augmented 5th. All three intervals within the chord are major 3rds.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: In a major key, what chord type naturally occurs on the 7th scale degree?
correct_answer: Half-diminished (minor 7 flat 5)
wrong_answer_1: Diminished 7th
wrong_answer_2: Minor 7th
wrong_answer_3: Dominant 7th
explanation: In a major key, the 7th degree produces a half-diminished chord (e.g. in C major: Bm7b5 = B-D-F-A).

question_text: What is a major 6th chord?
correct_answer: A major triad with a major 6th interval added
wrong_answer_1: A major triad with a minor 6th
wrong_answer_2: A dominant chord with an added 6th
wrong_answer_3: A major 7th chord with the 7th replaced by a 6th
explanation: A major 6th chord (e.g. C6 = C-E-G-A) adds the major 6th above the root to a major triad. It often substitutes for maj7 chords.
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is a fully diminished seventh chord (dim7)?
correct_answer: A diminished triad with a diminished 7th added
wrong_answer_1: A diminished triad with a minor 7th
wrong_answer_2: A minor triad with flatted 5th and 7th
wrong_answer_3: A major triad with a lowered root
explanation: A fully diminished 7th chord (e.g. B°7 = B-D-F-Ab) consists of stacked minor 3rds throughout, giving it a symmetrical, highly unstable quality.

question_text: What is 'economy of motion' in chord voicing?
correct_answer: Moving chord tones the shortest possible distance between chord changes
wrong_answer_1: Playing chords as rapidly as possible
wrong_answer_2: Using as few notes as possible in a chord
wrong_answer_3: Avoiding all chord inversions
explanation: Economy of motion means finding voicings where individual notes move minimally between chords, reducing hand movement and creating natural voice leading.

question_text: What is 'voice leading'?
correct_answer: The smooth, often step-wise movement of individual notes from one chord to the next
wrong_answer_1: The technique of assigning melodies to specific singers
wrong_answer_2: The process of doubling notes in choral harmony
wrong_answer_3: The technique of always playing chords as arpeggios
explanation: Good voice leading guides each note through smooth motion chord to chord, minimizing leaps and creating a naturally flowing sound.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What is a 'secondary dominant'?
correct_answer: A dominant 7th chord that resolves to a diatonic chord other than the tonic
wrong_answer_1: The dominant chord in the relative minor key
wrong_answer_2: A chord built on the 2nd scale degree
wrong_answer_3: A chord borrowed from the parallel minor
explanation: A secondary dominant (e.g. V7/II or V7/IV) temporarily treats a non-tonic chord as a local tonic by preceding it with its own dominant 7th.

question_text: What is the 'tonic function' of a chord?
correct_answer: A chord that provides a sense of rest, stability, or resolution
wrong_answer_1: A chord that creates tension demanding resolution
wrong_answer_2: A chord that sets up the dominant
wrong_answer_3: A chord that connects tonic to dominant
explanation: Tonic function chords (I, VI, III) provide stability and serve as goals of harmonic motion — they feel like 'home.'

question_text: What is the 'dominant function' of a chord?
correct_answer: A chord that creates tension strongly wanting to resolve to the tonic
wrong_answer_1: A chord of rest and stability
wrong_answer_2: A chord that prolongs the tonic
wrong_answer_3: A chord built on the 4th scale degree
explanation: Dominant function chords (V, VII) are harmonically unstable and create strong directional pull toward the tonic.

question_text: What is a 'pre-dominant' (subdominant function) chord?
correct_answer: A chord that moves toward the dominant and eventually to the tonic
wrong_answer_1: A chord that prolongs the dominant
wrong_answer_2: A chord of rest and full resolution
wrong_answer_3: The chord that creates the most tension
explanation: Pre-dominant chords (II, IV) sit between tonic and dominant in harmonic progressions, preparing and intensifying the arrival of the dominant.

question_text: What is a 'deceptive cadence'?
correct_answer: A cadence where the dominant unexpectedly resolves to VI instead of I
wrong_answer_1: A cadence ending on the dominant chord
wrong_answer_2: A cadence beginning on the subdominant
wrong_answer_3: A cadence that completely avoids resolution
explanation: In a deceptive cadence, the expected V-to-I resolution is 'deceived' — V moves to VI instead, creating surprise and prolonging the phrase.

question_text: What is a 'perfect authentic cadence' (PAC)?
correct_answer: A cadence from V to I, both in root position, with the tonic note in the melody
wrong_answer_1: A cadence from IV to I
wrong_answer_2: A cadence that ends on the dominant
wrong_answer_3: A cadence from II to V
explanation: The perfect authentic cadence is the strongest conclusive ending — V to I in root position with scale degree 1 in the highest voice.

question_text: What does 'back-cycling' mean in jazz harmony?
correct_answer: Adding dominant chords working backwards from a target chord by perfect fourths
wrong_answer_1: Cycling through all 12 keys in sequence
wrong_answer_2: Playing a harmonic progression in exact reverse
wrong_answer_3: Returning to the beginning of a musical form
explanation: Back-cycling inserts a chain of II-V pairs leading to a target chord, e.g. adding V7/V before V7 before I, creating forward harmonic motion.

question_text: What is 'tritone substitution' in jazz?
correct_answer: Replacing a dominant chord with a dominant chord whose root is a tritone away
wrong_answer_1: Replacing the tonic with a chord a tritone away
wrong_answer_2: Adding a tritone interval to any chord
wrong_answer_3: Playing the tritone interval in place of a chord
explanation: Tritone substitution replaces V7 with a dominant 7th chord a tritone away — both share the same critical tritone (3rd and 7th), allowing smooth bass-line motion by half step.

question_text: What is 'modal interchange' (also called borrowed chords)?
correct_answer: Using chords borrowed from a parallel major or minor key
wrong_answer_1: Changing the key signature mid-piece
wrong_answer_2: Borrowing chords from the relative minor only
wrong_answer_3: Playing simultaneously in two different modes
explanation: Modal interchange borrows chords from the parallel key (e.g. using iv minor in a major key) adding harmonic color without full modulation.

question_text: What is a 'half cadence'?
correct_answer: A cadence that ends on the dominant, creating an unresolved, questioning feeling
wrong_answer_1: A cadence that ends on the tonic
wrong_answer_2: A cadence from V to VI
wrong_answer_3: A cadence made up of only one chord
explanation: A half cadence ends on V, providing a momentary pause but leaving the phrase open — like a musical question awaiting an answer.

question_text: What is 'contrary motion' in voice leading?
correct_answer: Two voices moving in opposite directions simultaneously
wrong_answer_1: Two voices moving in the same direction by the same interval
wrong_answer_2: Two voices holding on the same note
wrong_answer_3: Two voices that never cross registers
explanation: Contrary motion (one voice ascending, another descending) is the strongest voice-leading technique as it creates independence and avoids parallel octaves or 5ths.
sources_and_notes: General music theory; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is 6/8 time and how is it felt?
correct_answer: Compound duple meter — 2 main beats per measure, each dividing into 3 eighth notes
wrong_answer_1: Simple triple meter with 6 equal beats
wrong_answer_2: A meter with 6 quarter notes per measure
wrong_answer_3: Duple meter where each beat divides into 2 eighth notes
explanation: In 6/8, there are 2 main beats per measure, each subdividing into 3 eighth notes (dotted-quarter pulse), making it compound duple meter.

question_text: What is a 'hemiola'?
correct_answer: A rhythmic effect where 3 beats are felt over 2 beats (or vice versa) through regrouping
wrong_answer_1: A type of syncopation using only quarter notes
wrong_answer_2: A rhythmic augmentation technique
wrong_answer_3: A rest equal to three beats
explanation: A hemiola temporarily shifts the perceived beat grouping — for example, 3 groups of 2 eighth notes over a 6/8 bar feel like 3 beats instead of 2.
sources_and_notes: General music theory; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: What is 'rhythmic augmentation'?
correct_answer: Doubling all note values in a theme, making it twice as slow
wrong_answer_1: Halving the duration of all note values
wrong_answer_2: Adding extra beats to a measure
wrong_answer_3: Shifting all notes off the beat
explanation: In rhythmic augmentation, every note is held twice as long, broadening and slowing the original theme.

question_text: What is 'rhythmic diminution'?
correct_answer: Halving all note values in a theme, making it twice as fast
wrong_answer_1: Doubling all note values
wrong_answer_2: Adding syncopation to a theme
wrong_answer_3: Changing the time signature mid-piece
explanation: In rhythmic diminution, each note is half as long as the original, speeding the theme while maintaining its rhythmic shape.

question_text: What is 'polyrhythm'?
correct_answer: The simultaneous use of two or more contrasting rhythmic patterns
wrong_answer_1: A single rhythm played at very high speed
wrong_answer_2: A rhythm with no defined meter
wrong_answer_3: A rhythm that uses only triplets throughout
explanation: Polyrhythm occurs when two different rhythmic patterns play simultaneously — for example, 3 against 2 across two voices.

question_text: In 4/4 time, what is an eighth-note triplet?
correct_answer: Three eighth notes played in the time of two eighth notes (one beat)
wrong_answer_1: Three quarter notes in a row
wrong_answer_2: A group of three measures
wrong_answer_3: Three beats with equal emphasis
explanation: A triplet divides a beat into three equal parts instead of two. An eighth-note triplet fits 3 notes into the space of 2 eighth notes.

question_text: What does 'alla breve' (cut time) mean?
correct_answer: A time signature of 2/2, with 2 half-note beats per measure
wrong_answer_1: Playing the piece at half the written speed
wrong_answer_2: Playing at double the written speed
wrong_answer_3: 4/4 played at a slow tempo
explanation: Cut time (alla breve) is 2/2 — 2 beats per measure with the half note as the beat unit. Music is felt in 2 rather than 4.

question_text: What is 'metric modulation'?
correct_answer: A tempo change where a new beat unit equals a previous subdivision
wrong_answer_1: A change from major to minor
wrong_answer_2: A change in dynamic level over time
wrong_answer_3: A gradual speeding up across a piece
explanation: In metric modulation, a subdivision of the current meter becomes the new beat, creating a mathematically precise, related tempo change.

question_text: What is a 'pickup' (anacrusis or upbeat)?
correct_answer: One or more notes occurring before the first full measure
wrong_answer_1: A note played louder than the surrounding notes
wrong_answer_2: The final note of a piece
wrong_answer_3: Any note that falls on the downbeat
explanation: A pickup (upbeat/anacrusis) appears before the first complete measure, creating forward momentum into the downbeat.

question_text: What musical term indicates a gradual increase in tempo?
correct_answer: Accelerando
wrong_answer_1: Ritardando
wrong_answer_2: Fermata
wrong_answer_3: Sostenuto
explanation: Accelerando (accel.) instructs the performer to gradually speed up the tempo.

question_text: What is a 'quintuplet'?
correct_answer: Five notes played in the time of four (or three)
wrong_answer_1: A group of five measures
wrong_answer_2: A chord containing five notes
wrong_answer_3: A five-beat time signature
explanation: A quintuplet divides a beat into 5 equal parts, creating a five-against-four or five-against-three feel.

question_text: In 5/4 time, which beat grouping is most commonly used?
correct_answer: 3+2 or 2+3 (three beats followed by two, or two followed by three)
wrong_answer_1: Always felt as 5 equal, undivided beats
wrong_answer_2: Always divided as 4+1
wrong_answer_3: Always felt as triplet groupings
explanation: 5/4 time is typically experienced as either 3+2 or 2+3 within each measure, giving it its distinctive asymmetric, limping feel.
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol Elements of Music; General music theory

question_text: What is 'cross-rhythm'?
correct_answer: Accents or note groupings that contradict the underlying metric pulse
wrong_answer_1: Playing in two different time signatures simultaneously
wrong_answer_2: A rhythm that uses only rests on downbeats
wrong_answer_3: A rhythm that repeats every other measure
explanation: Cross-rhythm creates metric tension by placing structural accents against the expected beat pattern, producing a sense of layered rhythmic conflict.

question_text: What is sonata form?
correct_answer: A three-part structure with an Exposition, Development, and Recapitulation
wrong_answer_1: A form with a theme followed by unlimited variations
wrong_answer_2: A two-part binary form expanded with a coda
wrong_answer_3: A form exclusively for vocal works
explanation: Sonata form: Exposition (themes introduced, often modulating to V), Development (themes explored in new keys), Recapitulation (themes return in tonic).

question_text: What is the 'exposition' section in sonata form?
correct_answer: The opening section where the main themes are introduced
wrong_answer_1: Where themes are developed and transformed
wrong_answer_2: The final section returning to the home key
wrong_answer_3: The optional slow introduction before the main form
explanation: The exposition presents the primary theme (tonic) and secondary theme (contrasting key, often the dominant), establishing the musical material.

question_text: What is 'theme and variations' form?
correct_answer: A form in which a theme is stated and then presented with changes in each variation
wrong_answer_1: A form with two contrasting themes that never return
wrong_answer_2: A form with no clear structural divisions
wrong_answer_3: A form limited to piano compositions
explanation: In theme and variations, a central melody is transformed repeatedly through changes in harmony, melody, rhythm, texture, or dynamics across each variation.

question_text: What is rondo form?
correct_answer: A form where a main theme (A) alternates with contrasting episodes (ABACADA...)
wrong_answer_1: A form that always has exactly three sections
wrong_answer_2: A form that repeats identical material throughout
wrong_answer_3: A form only found in piano sonatas
explanation: In rondo form, the refrain theme (A) returns after each contrasting episode (B, C, D...), creating a recurring, interspersed structure.

question_text: What is a fugue?
correct_answer: A contrapuntal composition where a subject is introduced then imitated by successive voices
wrong_answer_1: A piece written for solo instrument only
wrong_answer_2: A dance-based binary form
wrong_answer_3: A form built on a single continuous unaccompanied melody
explanation: A fugue opens with a subject in one voice; successive voices enter imitating it (the answer), while previous voices continue with countersubjects and free counterpoint.

question_text: What happens in the 'development' section of sonata form?
correct_answer: Themes are fragmented, transformed, and explored in multiple new keys
wrong_answer_1: New themes are introduced for the first time
wrong_answer_2: All themes return in the tonic key
wrong_answer_3: A coda brings the movement to a close
explanation: The development takes material from the exposition and subjects it to fragmentation, modulation, inversion, and harmonic instability before the recapitulation.

question_text: What is a 'motif' (or motive)?
correct_answer: A short musical idea — a few notes or a rhythm — that recurs and develops throughout a piece
wrong_answer_1: A full melodic phrase
wrong_answer_2: A complete chord progression
wrong_answer_3: A major section of a composition
explanation: A motif is the smallest recognizable unit of musical identity. Beethoven's opening four notes of his 5th Symphony is a classic example.

question_text: What is the 'recapitulation' in sonata form?
correct_answer: The final section where the opening themes return in the home tonic key
wrong_answer_1: The section that introduces the themes initially
wrong_answer_2: The section that develops and transforms the themes
wrong_answer_3: The optional slow introduction
explanation: The recapitulation restates both the primary and secondary themes in the tonic, providing harmonic resolution after the development's instability.

question_text: What is 'strophic' form?
correct_answer: A form where the same music is repeated for each new verse of text
wrong_answer_1: A form where each verse has unique music
wrong_answer_2: A form with verse, chorus, and bridge sections
wrong_answer_3: A through-composed form with no repeats
explanation: In strophic form, the same melody and harmony are used for every stanza of text — common in folk songs and hymns.

question_text: What is 'through-composed' form?
correct_answer: Music that develops new material continuously without repeating any section
wrong_answer_1: Music built entirely on repeated sections
wrong_answer_2: Music with exactly two distinct sections
wrong_answer_3: Music that only varies one theme throughout
explanation: Through-composed music is continuously developing — no section is repeated verbatim from beginning to end.
sources_and_notes: General music theory; Nahre Sol Elements of Music

question_text: What is a 'period' in music theory?
correct_answer: A complete musical thought made up of an antecedent phrase and a consequent phrase
wrong_answer_1: A single musical phrase
wrong_answer_2: An entire movement of a composition
wrong_answer_3: A repeating chord progression
explanation: A period consists of two related phrases: the antecedent (question) ends on an unstable harmony; the consequent (answer) concludes more definitively.

question_text: What is a 'plagal cadence'?
correct_answer: A cadence moving from IV to I
wrong_answer_1: A cadence from V to I
wrong_answer_2: A cadence ending on the dominant chord
wrong_answer_3: A cadence from II to V
explanation: The plagal cadence (IV-I), often called the 'Amen cadence,' has a gentle, hymn-like quality and is less conclusive than the authentic (V-I) cadence.

question_text: What is 'chord-scale theory' in jazz?
correct_answer: The concept that each chord has a corresponding scale or mode that fits over it
wrong_answer_1: A theory that all chords must come from the major scale
wrong_answer_2: A theory that scales and chords must share the same root
wrong_answer_3: A way to build chords by stacking scale tones only
explanation: Chord-scale theory matches specific scales or modes to each chord in a progression, guiding improvisers in their note choices.

question_text: In jazz improvisation, what is the difference between 'inside' and 'outside' playing?
correct_answer: Inside stays within the chord's harmony; outside deliberately uses notes outside the harmony for tension
wrong_answer_1: Inside means quiet playing; outside means loud playing
wrong_answer_2: Inside means playing the melody; outside means improvising freely
wrong_answer_3: Inside is jazz style; outside refers to classical style
explanation: Inside playing is consonant with the current harmony. Outside playing intentionally clashes against the chord for tension before resolving back in.

question_text: What is a 'chord substitution' in jazz?
correct_answer: Replacing one chord with another that serves a similar harmonic function
wrong_answer_1: Transposing a chord to a different key
wrong_answer_2: Adding extra notes (extensions) to a chord
wrong_answer_3: Removing a chord from a progression
explanation: Chord substitutions replace standard chords with functionally related alternatives, adding harmonic variety and sophistication.

question_text: What is the harmonic function of a II-V without resolving to I?
correct_answer: It creates forward momentum and tension even without arriving at the tonic
wrong_answer_1: It permanently replaces the tonic
wrong_answer_2: It cancels the function of both chords
wrong_answer_3: It always implies modulation to a new key
explanation: A II-V without resolution can act as a turnaround, imply a key temporarily, or delay the expected resolution for sustained tension.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What is a 'rootless voicing' in jazz piano?
correct_answer: A chord voicing that omits the root note, relying on the bass player to supply it
wrong_answer_1: A chord voicing with no 3rd
wrong_answer_2: A chord voicing with no 7th
wrong_answer_3: A single-note melody line
explanation: Rootless voicings (e.g. 3rd, 7th, 9th, 13th) create sophisticated jazz textures and leave space for the bassist, who handles the root.
sources_and_notes: Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is the II-V-I progression in a minor key?
correct_answer: Half-diminished II – dominant V7 (with b9) – minor I (min or minmaj7)
wrong_answer_1: Minor II – minor V – major I
wrong_answer_2: Major II – major V – minor I
wrong_answer_3: The same progression as in major but transposed down
explanation: In minor, the II is half-diminished, the V7 often uses the harmonic minor's raised 7th (adding b9 tension), and the I is a minor chord.

question_text: What is a 'walking bass line'?
correct_answer: A bass line in steady quarter notes that outlines chord tones connected by scalar or chromatic steps
wrong_answer_1: A bass line playing only root notes on beat 1
wrong_answer_2: A bass line that plays every other beat
wrong_answer_3: A bass line that never leaves the low register
explanation: A walking bass line moves in steady quarter notes, using chord tones, passing tones, and chromatic approaches to smoothly connect harmonies.
sources_and_notes: General music theory; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: What is a 'lead sheet'?
correct_answer: A simplified score showing melody, chord symbols, and lyrics
wrong_answer_1: A complete orchestral score
wrong_answer_2: A chart with only the bass line written out
wrong_answer_3: A chord chart with no melody
explanation: A lead sheet provides melody, harmony (chord symbols), and lyrics — the minimum information needed for musicians to interpret and arrange a piece.

question_text: What musical style is bebop?
correct_answer: A complex jazz style from the 1940s featuring fast tempos, intricate melodies, and advanced harmony
wrong_answer_1: A slow, simple jazz style designed for dancing
wrong_answer_2: An early 1920s New Orleans jazz style
wrong_answer_3: A modern style using purely modal harmony
explanation: Bebop (Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, Thelonious Monk) broke from dance music with virtuosic improvisation, rapid harmonic motion, and extended harmony.

question_text: What is a 'lick' in jazz?
correct_answer: A short, memorized melodic phrase or pattern used within an improvised solo
wrong_answer_1: A specific type of chord voicing
wrong_answer_2: A rhythmic groove pattern
wrong_answer_3: A type of scale pattern
explanation: A lick is a practiced melodic idea that an improviser can insert into a solo. Building a vocabulary of licks is a fundamental part of jazz learning.
sources_and_notes: General music theory; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What are 'guide tones' in jazz harmony?
correct_answer: The 3rd and 7th of a chord, which define its quality and essential sound
wrong_answer_1: The root and 5th of a chord
wrong_answer_2: The 9th and 13th extension tones
wrong_answer_3: The root and 3rd of a chord
explanation: Guide tones (3rd and 7th) determine whether a chord is major, minor, or dominant — they carry the chord's harmonic identity and form the tritone in dominant chords.
sources_and_notes: The Beato Book 4.0; Kent Hewitt Jazz Piano

question_text: Why is the pentatonic scale especially useful in jazz improvisation?
correct_answer: Its 5 notes avoid the most dissonant tones, making it compatible over multiple chords
wrong_answer_1: It is only used in minor key contexts
wrong_answer_2: It replaces the need to learn modes
wrong_answer_3: It works only over dominant 7th chords
explanation: The pentatonic scale omits scale tones that clash most with chord changes, allowing improvisers to play over multiple chords without clashing notes.
sources_and_notes: Nahre Sol Elements of Music; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is the blues scale?
correct_answer: A minor pentatonic scale with an added flatted 5th (b5), also known as the blue note
wrong_answer_1: The major scale used in blues contexts
wrong_answer_2: A scale with no half steps
wrong_answer_3: The natural minor scale
explanation: The blues scale adds the b5 (blue note) to the minor pentatonic, producing the characteristic tension and expressive quality central to blues and jazz.

question_text: How many sharps are in the key of A major?
explanation: A major has three sharps: F#, C#, and G#.

question_text: What are the three sharps in A major's key signature?
explanation: A major's key signature contains F#, C#, and G# — raising the 7th, 3rd, and 6th scale degrees respectively.

question_text: How many flats are in the key of Eb major?
explanation: Eb major has three flats: Bb, Eb, and Ab.

question_text: What is the relative minor of G major?
correct_answer: E minor
wrong_answer_1: B minor
wrong_answer_2: D minor
wrong_answer_3: A minor
explanation: The relative minor is on the 6th scale degree of the major scale. The 6th degree of G major is E, so E minor is the relative minor of G major (both share F# in the key signature).

question_text: How many sharps are in E major?
explanation: E major has four sharps: F#, C#, G#, D#.

question_text: What is the order in which sharps appear in key signatures?
correct_answer: F# C# G# D# A# E# B#
wrong_answer_1: C# F# G# D# A# E# B#
wrong_answer_2: F# G# D# C# A# E# B#
wrong_answer_3: B# E# A# D# G# C# F#
explanation: Sharps are added in a fixed order (F-C-G-D-A-E-B), often remembered with the mnemonic 'Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle.'
sources_and_notes: General music theory; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: What is the order in which flats appear in key signatures?
correct_answer: Bb Eb Ab Db Gb Cb Fb
wrong_answer_1: Eb Bb Ab Db Gb Cb Fb
wrong_answer_2: Ab Eb Bb Db Gb Cb Fb
wrong_answer_3: Bb Ab Eb Db Gb Cb Fb
explanation: Flats appear in the reverse order of sharps (B-E-A-D-G-C-F), remembered with 'Battle Ends And Down Goes Charles's Father.'
sources_and_notes: General music theory; The Beato Book 4.0

question_text: How many flats are in Ab major?
explanation: Ab major has four flats: Bb, Eb, Ab, and Db.

question_text: Which major key has 5 sharps?
correct_answer: B major
wrong_answer_1: E major
wrong_answer_2: F# major
wrong_answer_3: A major
explanation: B major has 5 sharps: F#, C#, G#, D#, A#.

question_text: What is the relative major of A minor?
correct_answer: C major
wrong_answer_1: F major
wrong_answer_2: G major
wrong_answer_3: E major
explanation: A minor is the relative minor of C major — both share the same key signature (no sharps or flats). To find the relative major, go up a minor 3rd from the minor tonic.

question_text: What is the parallel relationship between C major and C minor?
correct_answer: They share the same root note but have different key signatures and scale structures
wrong_answer_1: They share the same key signature
wrong_answer_2: They share the same 7th scale degree
wrong_answer_3: They always sound identical in character
explanation: C major (no accidentals) and C minor (3 flats: Bb, Eb, Ab) start on the same pitch but have entirely different key signatures and emotional qualities.

question_text: How many sharps are in F# major?
explanation: F# major has 6 sharps: F#, C#, G#, D#, A#, E#.

question_text: What is the Lydian Dominant scale, and what chord type does it serve?
correct_answer: Mode 4 of melodic minor; serves dominant 7th chords with a raised 11th (#11)
wrong_answer_1: Mode 4 of harmonic minor; serves major 7th chords
wrong_answer_2: Mode 5 of melodic minor; serves minor 7th chords
wrong_answer_3: Mode 6 of major scale; serves augmented chords
explanation: Lydian Dominant (1, 2, 3, #4, 5, 6, b7) is mode 4 of melodic minor. Its raised 4th creates a #11 over the dominant, making it ideal for chords like C7#11.

question_text: What is the 'Altered Scale' (Super Locrian), and over what chord type is it used?
correct_answer: Mode 7 of melodic minor; used over altered dominant chords (7b9, 7#9, 7b13, 7#11)
wrong_answer_1: Mode 7 of harmonic minor; used over half-diminished chords
wrong_answer_2: Mode 4 of major scale with a flatted 7th
wrong_answer_3: A natural minor scale with all tones lowered
explanation: The Altered Scale (Superlocrian) is mode 7 of melodic minor. It contains every possible alteration (b9, #9, b5, #11, b13), making it the ultimate scale for altered dominant chords.

question_text: What distinguishes Phrygian Dominant from the regular Phrygian mode?
correct_answer: Phrygian Dominant has a major 3rd (natural 3rd) instead of a minor 3rd, and is mode 5 of harmonic minor
wrong_answer_1: Phrygian Dominant has a raised 7th instead of a flatted 7th
wrong_answer_2: Phrygian Dominant has a raised 6th instead of a flatted 6th
wrong_answer_3: Phrygian Dominant and regular Phrygian are identical scales
explanation: Phrygian Dominant (1, b2, 3, 4, 5, b6, b7) is mode 5 of harmonic minor. The major 3rd gives it a Spanish/flamenco and Middle Eastern character, unlike Phrygian's minor 3rd.

question_text: What is the whole-tone scale?
correct_answer: A 6-note scale composed entirely of whole steps with no half steps
wrong_answer_1: A 7-note scale using all whole steps
wrong_answer_2: An 8-note scale alternating whole and half steps
wrong_answer_3: A scale with no consistent interval pattern
explanation: The whole-tone scale has 6 notes (e.g. C-D-E-F#-G#-A#), with only whole steps throughout. It creates an ambiguous, floating quality and fits augmented and dominant b5/#5 chords.
